title: Why Money is Freedom: Essential Wealth Strategies Every Woman Needs to Know
guestBio: Melissa Northway is a Financial Educator and Wealth Strategist with True Path Financial and former President of NABO California. She's passionate about bringing high-net-worth financial strategies to everyday women and families, helping them protect their assets, reduce taxes, and build generational wealth.
excerpt: Financial strategist Melissa Northway reveals why women disproportionately end up poor in retirement and shares the wealth-building secrets typically reserved for high-net-worth individuals. This episode will transform how you think about money as a tool for freedom.
metaDescription: Learn wealth strategies for women from financial expert Melissa Northway on cash value life insurance, tax planning & financial freedom.
keyTakeaways: Money equals freedom—the freedom to live life without financial worry and make choices based on values, not limitations.
Women need to take control of their financial planning instead of leaving it to their partners, as statistics show elderly women are disproportionately poor.
Your greatest asset is yourself—protect your earning potential with proper insurance and living benefit riders that provide access to death benefits while alive.
Cash value life insurance functions as a Tier 1 asset with a zero floor, meaning you can't lose money while building tax-advantaged wealth.
Asset swap strategies used by billionaires like Zuckerberg can be adapted for everyday women to optimize tax efficiency.
Developing AI skills can increase income by 10-25%, making it crucial for women to embrace technology for financial advancement.
Approaching money with a 'beginner's mind' and being coachable allows you to learn strategies that can transform your financial future.

pullQuotes.text: Money is freedom—freedom to live the life you want without financial worry

timestamps.label: Why Women Don't Talk About Money
The Statistics: Why Women End Up Poor

content:
What if everything you've been told about money is wrong? What if the key to your freedom isn't working harder, saving every penny, or cutting back on small luxuries—but instead understanding money as a tool for liberation?

In this transformative episode of The Manage Her Podcast, host Aimee Rickabus sits down with financial strategist Melissa Northway to shatter conventional wisdom about women and wealth. Melissa brings a refreshing perspective: money isn't the root of all evil or something to feel guilty about accumulating. Instead, money is freedom—the freedom to live life on your terms, make choices based on your values rather than your bank account, and create the impact you're meant to have in this world.

The Uncomfortable Truth About Women and Money

Melissa doesn't sugarcoat the reality: women are disproportionately poor in their later years, and it's not because they're bad with money. The statistics are sobering, but they're also a wake-up call. Too many women have abdicated financial responsibility to their partners, assuming someone else will take care of their future security. This mindset isn't just dangerous—it's disempowering.

The conversation reveals why women don't talk about money the way men do. While men discuss investments and strategies on the golf course, women often feel uncomfortable even mentioning their financial goals. This silence perpetuates a cycle of financial illiteracy that keeps women dependent and vulnerable. It's time to break that cycle.

Melissa emphasizes that changing your relationship with money starts with changing your mindset. You have permission to want wealth. You have permission to talk about money openly. You have permission to learn strategies that have traditionally been reserved for the wealthy elite. Your financial education is not selfish—it's essential.

Beyond Budgeting: Real Wealth-Building Strategies

This episode goes far beyond typical financial advice about budgeting and expense tracking. Melissa introduces concepts that might sound foreign but are incredibly powerful: cash value life insurance, living benefit riders, asset swaps, and tax-advantaged wealth accumulation strategies. These aren't just for millionaires—they're tools that everyday women can and should understand and utilize.

Cash value life insurance, for instance, functions as what Melissa calls a "Tier 1 asset" with a zero floor. This means your money can grow but never decrease, providing both protection and wealth accumulation in one vehicle. It's a concept that challenges traditional thinking about life insurance as merely a death benefit and reframes it as a living financial tool.

The discussion of living benefit riders is particularly compelling. These riders allow you to access your death benefit while you're still alive if you become critically ill or injured. Melissa shares real stories of clients who have utilized these benefits, transforming what could have been financial catastrophes into manageable situations with dignity intact.

Protecting Your Greatest Asset

One of the most powerful concepts Melissa presents is the idea that you are your greatest asset. Your ability to earn income, to create value, to contribute to your family and community—this is what needs protection above all else. Yet most people spend more time insuring their cars than protecting their earning potential.

This perspective shift is crucial for women, who often prioritize everyone else's needs before their own financial security. Melissa gives you permission to invest in protecting yourself, to see personal financial protection not as selfish but as responsible stewardship of your family's future.

The asset swap strategies she discusses—the same techniques used by billionaires like Mark Zuckerberg when selling Facebook stock—demonstrate how sophisticated financial planning can optimize tax efficiency and wealth preservation. These aren't mysterious secrets reserved for the ultra-wealthy; they're learnable strategies that can benefit anyone willing to understand them.

The Future of Women and Wealth

Melissa's work extends beyond traditional financial planning. She's co-founding the National Association for Women in AI (NAWAI), recognizing that developing AI skills can increase income by 10-25%. This forward-thinking approach acknowledges that financial freedom in the future will require embracing technological advancement, not fearing it.

The conversation touches on international strategies as well, including retirement planning that considers geographic arbitrage—how living in countries like Portugal could save hundreds of thousands of dollars over a retirement lifetime while providing a higher quality of life.

Your Permission Slip to Financial Freedom

Perhaps the most important takeaway from this episode is the permission it gives you to approach money with what Melissa calls a "beginner's mind." You don't have to have all the answers right now. You don't have to understand every financial concept perfectly before you start. What you need is the willingness to be coachable and the recognition that today is the best day to start planning for tomorrow.

Melissa's approach is refreshingly practical and shame-free. She acknowledges that many people have complicated relationships with money—whether from childhood conditioning, religious teachings, or societal messaging. But she also provides a clear path forward: education, strategy, and action.

This episode isn't just about money—it's about freedom, choice, and the power to create the life you truly want. It's about recognizing that financial literacy isn't a luxury or something you'll get to eventually. It's a necessity for every woman who wants to live fully, love deeply, and leave a lasting impact on the world.

The time for financial passivity is over. The time for taking control of your financial future is now. As Melissa reminds us, money is simply a tool—but in the right hands, with the right knowledge, it becomes a tool for transformation, not just for yourself, but for generations to come.

quiz.title: What's Your Money Freedom Archetype?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach wealth and financial independence. Your mindset shapes your money story—let's uncover yours.

questions.question: When you think about your financial future, what drives you most?

options.text: The feeling that there are opportunities I haven't discovered yet
Making sure I'm protected from worst-case scenarios
Creating enough wealth to live completely on my terms
Building a solid foundation I can count on long-term

questions.question: How do you typically react when someone shares an unconventional money strategy?

options.text: I'm intrigued and want to understand the principles behind it
I'm skeptical until I can thoroughly research the risks
I'm excited if it could accelerate my wealth-building goals
I want to see the long-term track record before considering it

questions.question: What's your biggest frustration with traditional financial advice?

options.text: It feels too generic and doesn't address my unique situation
It often involves more risk than I'm comfortable with
It's too conservative and slow for my timeline
It's not specific enough about the step-by-step process

questions.question: When facing a major financial decision, you tend to:

options.text: Trust your gut feeling after gathering some key information
Research extensively and consult multiple trusted sources
Focus on the potential upside and move quickly if it aligns with your goals
Create a detailed analysis of pros, cons, and long-term implications

questions.question: How do you feel about the statement 'money is freedom'?

options.text: It resonates deeply—I've always sensed money was about more than just security
I agree, but I worry about the risks involved in pursuing wealth
Absolutely—that's exactly why I'm focused on building significant wealth
I agree, and I want a clear plan to systematically build that freedom

questions.question: What would motivate you most to learn advanced wealth strategies?

options.text: Discovering approaches that feel aligned with your values and intuition
Finding strategies that offer growth while protecting your downside
Learning tactics that successful people use to accelerate wealth building
Understanding proven systems you can implement consistently over time

results.title: The Intuitive Builder
results.description: You have a natural sense that there's more to money than what you've been taught, and you're ready to explore beyond traditional advice. You trust your instincts and aren't afraid to question conventional wisdom when it doesn't align with your values. Your openness to new perspectives is your superpower—you just need the right strategies to match your intuitive approach. This episode will give you the advanced wealth-building tools that feel as sophisticated as your instincts.

results.title: The Security Seeker
results.description: You value stability above all else and prefer to make careful, well-researched financial decisions. Your cautious approach has likely served you well, but you may be missing opportunities for growth by playing it too safe. Your methodical nature is actually perfect for implementing the systematic wealth strategies Melissa shares. This episode will show you how to build security AND growth using proven strategies that align with your careful approach.

results.title: The Ambitious Optimist
results.description: You're naturally drawn to big goals and aren't afraid to take calculated risks to achieve them. Your confidence is inspiring, and you intuitively understand that money is a tool for creating the life you want. You're ready to level up but need the sophisticated strategies to match your ambitious vision. This episode will give you the billionaire-level tactics and mindset shifts to accelerate your wealth-building journey.

results.title: The Steady Strategist
results.description: You believe in consistent, disciplined approaches to building wealth over time. You're practical and realistic about money, preferring proven methods to flashy trends. Your steady nature is actually ideal for the long-term wealth strategies that create lasting financial freedom. This episode will show you how to optimize your systematic approach with advanced techniques that compound your steady efforts into significant wealth.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manager, the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author of The Manager, entrepreneur, and mama of six. Today's guest is someone who's helping women reclaim their financial power, not through hustle, but through education and strategy.

Melissa Northway is a financial educator and wealth strategist with True Path Financial and the former president of NABO California, where she's been a champion for women in business and leadership. Melissa's mission is simple but radical: to empower women with money to understand it, talk about it, and use it as a tool for freedom. She helps individuals, families, and her firm even helps professional athletes build financial security through smart tax strategies, charitable giving, and innovative tools like cash value life insurance and asset swaps that protect wealth while it grows.

In this episode, we're talking about how to change your relationship with money, how to protect what you've built, and how to create real financial freedom. Not someday, but starting right now. Welcome to the show, Melissa. I'm so glad you're here.

**Melissa:** Thank you so much. I'm so excited. And thank you. That was a great intro. I was like, who was that lady you're talking about?

**Aimee:** That lady is you. You take that and you own that.

**Melissa:** So, Melissa, you've said that money equals freedom. What do you mean by that? And how has your own relationship with money evolved over time?

**Melissa:** It's interesting because I'll do webinars or presentations and I always ask the women in the audience what is their relationship with money or what's the first word that comes to mind when I say the word money. And I just find that so often women are afraid of it. They don't feel like they're wise enough to be investing. And so I see myself in some of those women because I think of myself when I was younger and I don't think I had the respect that I have now for money and what money represents.

For me, I'll ask women one, what emotion or thought or word comes to mind when I say money, I hear all these interesting responses. And then the other one is how does it make you feel? And so my relationship has changed over time with my knowledge and I really feel like in the last six months I've gotten a master class in finances and wealth building with the firm True Path. I'm pretty much the only woman at the firm. And it's just been such a wonderful experience because I want to now bring this information and knowledge to other women and families and business owners to help empower them. So I think money is freedom. Freedom to live the life that you want to live without having to worry about money.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Absolutely. As a former president of NABO California, you've been such a force for women in business. What did you notice about how women talk or don't talk about money at that level?

**Melissa:** Gosh, I almost feel at most levels, right? From the business owners to everyday moms, which we both know is the hardest job on the planet, to just everyday friends. It's not something that women talk about. And I feel like I want to shout from the rooftops that you want to talk about money. You want to talk about what do you want for your own life? How is money going to help you achieve your goals?

And so I just feel like it's something that women need to be having these conversations because statistics, the majority of the elderly poor are women, and so often women for whatever reason, their family dynamics, cultural dynamics, they leave the planning to their men or partners. And I just want to say, hey, listen, you need to be vested. You need to know where things are. You need to make sure maybe your partner or husband needs to have help, right? And he's trying to be macho and he's like, "Well, I got it." Well, maybe he doesn't have it. Maybe he needs some support.

But I think women need to really be a lot more mindful on having this conversation. When you and I when we first met, we were so driven by that. We're so excited about it. And it's not because of what it buys so much as what it can do for somebody in your life and your family, but also your community.

**Aimee:** Absolutely. Because the more you make, the more you can help your community empower. Yeah. The impact. Exactly. So, I think it's important. We need to have these conversations. Men do it on the golf course all the time.

**Melissa:** They sure do. I know. They sit around and talk about financial planning and tax strategies. And I would love it if we could change the culture of women where we could have those same conversations. I really do. With my best friend, we do talk about these kinds of things, but she's one of the only women that I can talk money with.

**Aimee:** Yes. And I think that I'd love it if talking money became more of a thing that women could have in their conversation, within our wheelhouse, feeling comfortable talking about who we're using for financial planning and 401ks and self-directed Roth IRAs and all of these cool things that we should really know about because if you're making money, you might as well be saving money.

**Melissa:** Well, saving money and making sure that your money is working for you, right? And getting an understanding. So I would just recommend to your viewers, your audience, really dive deep into what is your relationship with money? And if money makes you anxious or you just feel like you don't know enough, dive deep into that, sit with that and then it's like, okay, well what do I want at the end game?

So I'm a little different. I'll say okay, what's the end game for you? What do you want retirement to look like? How much money do you want to have come through that? And then are we on track for hitting those numbers? Look at it less as something that is burdensome or scary and more like, hey, I want these things in my life, so I'm going to make and do whatever I have to do to make it happen.

But why do you think, Aimee, why do you think women don't have these conversations? Because I've actually had women tell me and got upset with me. They're like, "Well, I don't really want to talk about that." And I was like, "Oh, geez. Well, I sure do. What time is it?"

**Aimee:** I know. Well, I think that there's a cultural component. I don't think our mothers generally talked about money. So, we learn a lot of our behavior and conversation or social skills from our mothers who learned them from their mothers. I think it was impolite for women to talk about money for a long time.

But now we're in a place where there are a lot of women that will never marry. Those women, these millennial women that are inheriting this $17 trillion right now are going to need to know how to speak money. They're going to need to know how to talk tax deferment and they're going to need to know how to invest their money wisely in real estate and other vehicles. Or else if you don't take care of your money, it will disappear on you.

But if you plant your money like seeds, it will grow and you can just eat the fruit off your trees and your principle will stay the same and you can just live off of the interest if you do things wisely. I just happened to be so lucky that one of the women that was so influential in my life was a little old lady named Jean.

**Melissa:** Yeah, I know. I heard that.

**Aimee:** And she was the controller of the Wilshire Country Club. And she was so smart about money. And I was so lucky at such a young age to have someone who was talking to me about interest and investments and all these things that I don't think anybody else my age was probably having these kinds of conversations. She was really ahead of her time. Honestly, she really was.

**Melissa:** And who taught her? Was she self-taught?

**Aimee:** She was self-taught. I think she started at the Wilshire Country Club as a secretary and basically was just sharp as a whip and just worked her way up all the way up to the top person in accounting which is the controller. And she was just smart as a whip and so good with money and just I really honestly I want to be like Jean when I grow up. I do. She's just amazing.

**Melissa:** But how lucky you are that she came into your life and I know you gave a lot to her. It was a beautiful mutual relationship, friendship just from what you had shared with me. But I think that I hear so often people, men and women, and these are people, high earners, they're like, "Nobody taught me about this or how come more people don't know about this?" And so, it really is, I feel like, a lifelong journey of learning about money and again, understand your relationship with it. And then surround yourself with people that you trust.

**Aimee:** Yes. And make it your mission to control it because if you don't control it, it's going to control how you live your life and how you end up.

**Melissa:** Yeah, absolutely. Who wants to end up homeless and be a statistic just because you didn't tend your garden? Life is, I'm realizing as I'm writing my next book that there's all of these things that we have to tend in life and money happens to be one of the fields that we need to be tending and that we need to be okay with the fact that that does need tending because at some point we're going to stop working and we want it.

Maybe we want to have impact or maybe we just want to have time with our family so we can be grandma and be comfortable enough to be a really awesome grandma that can pick our grandkids up from school and maybe teach them. My kids are like, "Will you teach my kids? Will you be their homeschool teacher when we have kids?" And I'm like, "Yeah, I want to do that. That's what I want to do when I grow up."

**Melissa:** Yeah, that's right.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I'd love to do that when I grow up. So, I mean, I think of where we want to be and all the things, all of the fields we need to tend to get to where we want to end up. And money being a very important field.

**Melissa:** It is. I mean, it has the potential to have a lot of impact. And I'm always surprised. I have heard this in my line of work that they feel that money is evil. And I was like, "Wow, how sad to have that," obviously that noise came from somewhere, but to think that money, of course in the wrong hands or not doing good, but it has the potential to do so so much good.

**Aimee:** It really does. I really do because I see in fact the thing I think the thing that led to the success that we've really experienced in the last five years in the businesses was me deciding that I wanted to have a big impact in the nonprofit world. I wanted to have, my goal was I want to donate $100,000 a year to nonprofits. And once I kind of put, wrapped my head around what I wanted to do, my world grew to meet that level.

**Melissa:** That's so wonderful.

**Aimee:** You know, just deciding you want to make an impact.

**Melissa:** Yes. And whatever level of impact that is. I mean, I think women are very hard on themselves about so many things, right? But we get a lot of weird messaging in our society, right? You can't be too successful, you can't be too much. So much of that. If you do want to make an impact from here to here, whatever, take steps to get to that point where you can have this kind of impact and I think once you visualize that and I'm such a big believer of you've got to visualize that and then it's going to happen, but you've got to see yourself there.

**Aimee:** Totally. And get down to the granular details of really where you want to be. When I get really honest about this, I'll be like, "Oh, I want to do this." And it's like, "Okay, Aimee, but in order to do this, let's get really specific. I want specific numbers around all of these aspects." And once I lay down exactly how I'm going to grow something, it just happens.

**Melissa:** Yeah. Well, I feel like the universe aligns. You meet the right people. Well, you know, even today I met that lady who works with women venture capitalists who only invest in women IT companies and here we are launching, so the universe will definitely align and coming from a pure heart. I think that's really important.

**Aimee:** And pulling out the subconscious mind and bringing it up to the conscious mind, too. Sometimes we want things, but they're on the way back of your brain, and you got to pull them to the front of your brain where you can really, I really think you have to visualize them in the front of your mind. And a lot of times, my husband and I would, I mean, back in the olden days, 15 years ago, we would sit down and make vision boards together.

**Melissa:** Oh, I love that.

**Aimee:** And it's been amazing to see how we've manifested the exact thing that was on our vision board. His car is the exact thing that was on our vision board. My car is the exact thing. So, it's kind of amazing how you can really do this, but you really have to be conscious about it. So, it's becoming conscious about, hey, I really want to learn to be better with money. I want to have better internal dialogue with myself about money.

**Melissa:** Yeah. There's so much I feel with women particularly, there's a lot of fear around money. And feeling like, I'm going to make a mistake or then listening to people and no offense, but if your family member hasn't built an empire, wouldn't it be smarter to listen to a firm or listen to people who have been doing this 20, 30 years and have built an empire. Not to say that we all love our family members, but be mindful of where we're getting our information because there is a lot of information out there and a lot of it's incorrect.

**Aimee:** It's so true. It changes all the time, too. I feel like there's a continuing education part of this thing because there are always new vehicles. There are always changes in tax codes and new loopholes and all those things. So, it's something you might have known, but you probably don't know everything all the way up to date. I feel like we need to learn until it's our time or should I hire somebody who does?

**Melissa:** If you don't have it, you outsource it to somebody who is up to date on all these things because I mean I loved when I was a real estate investor in my 20s. I loved all of the tax loopholes for real estate. It was impressive how many loopholes there were with real estate. I never had to pay taxes. It was so fun.

**Aimee:** You know, it is fun.

**Melissa:** It's fun. Money should be fun. Yeah. Instead of scary. Yeah. It should be fun to do these things and be able to afford the lifestyle that you want.

**Aimee:** But no, you're absolutely right. People don't know that about real estate, land, oil. There's all these tax codes and laws, even investments. There's certain investments you can invest in that are tax deferred. It's really, really cool. It's fun to learn about. And once you surround yourself with experts, I had a CPA who was an expert in real estate. And for me, I knew we were in the safety zone with everything we did above the line. And I thought, man, this is the coolest thing ever. Why don't more people know about this? Why doesn't everybody do this?

**Melissa:** Yeah. And I think it's just information because we get that, even what we shared with you with Ryan, we get a lot of why don't more people do this? And it's usually because a lot of these strategies is geared toward people who have high net worth, right? And so I feel like it's my mission is to bring this to the everyday person. These are strategies that are available. You don't have to be $10 million net worth in order to get this information and knowledge.

But yeah, getting yourself out there and know enough where you're comfortable, I guess. But definitely, I think it is important. It's like we go to a doctor, we're not going to tell the doctor how to do surgery on us. Right. We need to trust them to a certain level that they're going to do their job that they spent the last 10 years studying. Same kind of concept is work with experts who can empower you and lift you to where you want to go.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Educate you and know more than you do on these things. You don't have to, I know this was my big thing was I never really needed to know everything. I just needed to know someone who knew it.

**Melissa:** Yes, that's true. Right. Yeah. Yeah. And just be open. It's interesting because we kind of say that in our industry, you'll have three different types of personalities. The ones who go in who are very much open to it, I would say they're coachable. And then the ones who are immediately closed off, right? And then no matter what, and then the third is no matter what advice you give them, they're still going to do what already was in their head. So, they just kind of went through the exercise of consulting.

But I think the biggest advice I would give to people, which I think goes with anything in life, is be coachable. Be willing to consume new information, what makes sense to you, what feels right, but be able to trust that these people know what they're doing. And have that faith.

**Aimee:** Yeah. I love it. Well, in yoga, we call it the beginner's mind. And I love it. When you go into something new, you can go with a beginner's mind. Like, I don't know anything about this. It'll be a fun adventure for me to learn about this.

**Melissa:** Right. Right. Yeah. But I think women, nothing against men, half my clients are men, but I think women have less of an ego going into it. They don't have that we should know everything energy. I don't think most women don't, right? And so we'll see some a little bit more resistance from the men of well, I should know this. Why don't I know that? And then it's like an attack. No, no. Hey, this is information. Like you said, laws change all the time, tax codes. Let's just be open to receiving the information and see if it makes sense for you.

**Aimee:** Yeah, absolutely. Yeah. So, keep an open mind. Talk to people and then find the people that you trust. Go ahead and talk to a bunch of people. Talk to a bunch of financial planners, advisors, go meet your people. Go find your people. That's the big advice I would say to people is don't be afraid to, you don't have to settle with the first person you talk to. If it's not a good fit, keep looking. Because there are a lot of people that do this. So, finding the person who understands you and can speak to you and that you trust and that you feel good about is the key.

**Melissa:** And you're good. I agree. I agree. Yeah. I've heard a lot of women, a lot of times they like working with a woman in a financial planner just because they don't feel like there's that condescending and I'm not saying because the women in my office are amazing. But I think some women do feel like or they're just talking to the husband and they're not even talking to the wife.

**Aimee:** Yeah, that's a little old school. I feel like the younger generation doesn't do that as much. I had we had a meeting once where we were working with a web designer and he was, here we were working on our company's website and the guy is talking straight to my husband and I got out of the meeting and I was like, that is not going to be our web designer. Yeah. Exactly. I was like, how dumb is that? Right. It's like you always talk to both. I was like, "Let me tell you one thing that has not happened." So, needless to say, we didn't work with them.

**Melissa:** You want to feel like you're on an equal footing with someone that there's a mutual respect for one another. That's when you work with people.

**Aimee:** Yes. Yes. That's a biggie. Yeah. It's a big one. It's a big one for me. It's always a big one for me. I'm not going to work with somebody who can't have mutual respect.

**Melissa:** Yeah. Absolutely. And I think that's beautiful. So, let's talk about cash value life insurance called a tier one asset. What does it mean and how can women use it to create long-term financial stability?

So, a tier one asset is an asset that is, like a treasury bond, CDs. Banks have to have a certain percentage of their assets on their books need to be tier one, which is considered very safe. And that all happened after 2008. They need to have a certain proportion. So, cash value life insurance is considered a tier one asset. And it's because you don't have the risk of losing money. We have what's called a zero floor. You are mirroring. Let's say for example, we're mirroring the S&P. If the market goes up, whatever amount we had in our cash value life insurance, we get credited. That will go up. If the market goes, if it's not doing well for the year and it goes down 30%, 40%, we don't lose any money. We have what's called a zero floor. So, it's considered a tier one asset. It's considered very safe.

What I love about cash life insurance, couple things. One, you can borrow from the accumulated cash. I love to tell the story that this is how Walt Disney got Disneyland started.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Melissa:** He borrowed from his cash value life insurance policy because nobody could see his vision. What do you mean an amusement park headed up by a duck and a mouse? People couldn't see his vision. So, he borrowed from his policy to get it started enough where he built enough where the investors were like, "Oh, okay. I see the vision now."

So, people can borrow or it can be a loan or a withdrawal and you don't have to pay it back upon your death. The death benefit would pay back your loan. But this is a really great way to become your own bank.

**Aimee:** Wow.

**Melissa:** Because basically when you pay that money back, let's say you buy property. You want to buy some property. You borrowed from yourself. You went and bought that property. You fixed it up. You turned around. You sold it. You made money on it. You put it back into the policy, all that money that you had taken out, it gets credited. So, you still get credited, it's still making money even though it technically wasn't there.

**Aimee:** Wow, that's very interesting.

**Melissa:** So, you become your own bank. You hear people say become your own bank. That's basically what they mean. So, cash life insurance is considered one of the safest assets out there. I love it, too, because of the living benefit riders. I think you and I talked a little bit about that.

**Aimee:** Yeah, let's talk a little bit about that. I'm sure my people are like, "Wait, what?" A living benefit rider. What is that?

**Melissa:** Living benefit rider.
